figures.caption: Table 3: L1 context ablation on DUD-E (5 seeds, paired t-test with Bonferroni correction). “Generic L1” uses zero embedding at inference; “Correct L1” uses target-specific learned embedding. ∆ = Correct − Generic. See Appendix Table 10 for extended metrics with confidence intervals.

Table 10: Detailed per-target results from V3-baseline model (trained without target-specific L1 context). Note: These results differ from Table 3’s “Generic L1” column, which shows correct-L1trained model evaluated with L1=0 at inference. V3-baseline was trained without L1 information, achieving mean 0.839 AUC.

Table 19: ChEMBL–DUD-E overlap. “ChEMBL Train” shows total records (all activity values); per-target RF training uses only compounds meeting active threshold (pIC50 ≥ 6.0), e.g., CYP3A4 has 5,504 total records but only 67 actives. Active leakage is severe (mean 50%).

Figure 3: Attribution Analysis. (A) Integrated gradients for Celecoxib across 5 L1 contexts; yaxis shows atom index (0–25), colors indicate importance magnitude. Different contexts highlight different substructures. (B) Cosine similarity between attribution vectors drops from 0.999 (L0-only) to 0.878 (NESTDRUG), confirming context-specific explanations.

Figure 1: NESTDRUG Architecture. MPNN (L0) encodes molecules into 512-dim embeddings. Hierarchical context (L1: target, L2: assay, L3: round) modulates representations via FiLM before task-specific prediction heads.

Figure 5: L2/L3 Context Ablation (Negative Results). (A) L2 assay ablation shows no significant effect (mean ∆ = −0.006); L2 embeddings were not trained with real assay type annotations. (B) L3 temporal ablation shows no significant effect (mean ∆ = −0.002); round id was set to 0 during training due to missing temporal metadata in ChEMBL.
Table 8: Few-shot L1 adaptation hurts performance. Zero-shot (generic L1) outperforms adapted L1 on all targets. “Correct L1” shows the ceiling with full fine-tuning.
Figure 4: DMTA Replay. (A) Hit rates: model 75–88% vs. random 40–52%. (B) Enrichment 1.5–1.9×. (C) 29–55% fewer experiments. (D) NESTDRUG achieves 1.60× mean enrichment.
Table 14: DMTA replay results. Model selection achieves 1.5–1.9× enrichment over random, reducing experiments needed by 29–55%.
Table 7: FiLM γ parameters vary systematically by target family. Values show mean ± std across 512 dimensions.
Table 18: 1-NN Tanimoto similarity achieves near-perfect AUC without any learning, proving DUD-E structural bias.
Table 17: Per-target ChEMBL RF vs NESTDRUG (with correct L1). CYP3A4 (67 training actives) demonstrates multi-task transfer value.

Table 2: Per-target DUD-E results (ROC-AUC). NESTDRUG values use correct L1 context. “DUDE RF” = standard RF baseline trained on DUD-E train split only; differs from ChEMBL-trained “Per-target RF” in Table 1/17 (0.875 AUC).

Figure 2: Main Results. (A) Per-target ROC-AUC comparing NESTDRUG to baselines. (B) L1 ablation: correct (target-specific) vs. generic (zero) embeddings showing +5.7 pp mean improvement. (C) Ablation by model variant—V1: L0-only backbone without context; V3: full model with L1 context. (D) Mean AUC comparison to prior DUD-E methods.
